query: major mechanism of action of nitrates in acute angina

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Routine use of nitrates in patients with STEMI is not a strong recommendation unless used for angina relief. [7] [46] Nitrates should be used in patients with STEMI during the acute phase for the treatment of chest pain due to ischemia, treatment of acute hypertension, or as a vasodilator for treatment of acute LV failure. Contraindications include the use of phosphodiesterase inhibitors in the past 48 hours, marked hypotension with systolic blood pressure less than 90 mmHg, and suspected right ventricular (RV) infarction. The agent used in the acute phase is nitroglycerin. The formulation is usually sublingual, but an intravenous formulation is an option in patients with waxing and waning chest pain and patients with acute LV failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrates: Intravenous nitrates are more effective than sublingual nitrates with regard to symptom relief and regression of ST depression (NSTEMI). The dose is titrated upward until symptoms are relieved, blood pressure is normalized in hypertensive patients, or side effects such as a headache and hypotension are noted. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrates -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrates were first discovered in 1847 as a treatment for chest pain. [1] [2] The type of nitrates used and the administration route differs depending on the type of cardiovascular disease. The purpose of nitrate use is multifaceted. For patients with stable and predictable angina, long-acting nitrate can be used as prophylaxis, increasing exercise tolerance in patients. [3] This effect can be beneficial since patients with coronary artery disease tend to have other co-morbidities. For patients with acute anginal pain, short-acting nitrates are useful for symptom relief. [3] The use of nitrates is indicated in the following cardiovascular diseases: angina pectoris, acute coronary syndrome, arterial hypertension, and heart failure. [1] [2] The main types of nitrates are as followed [4] :

retrieved_docs.title: Angina -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Another mechanism by which anginal symptoms can be treated is vascular smooth muscle relaxation. This leads to coronary artery dilatation, thereby increasing perfusion ability. The drugs that work on this mechanism are dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, nitrates, and nicorandil. [1] Another drug used for chronic stable angina is ranolazine , which inhibits the late sodium current in ventricular myocardial cells. This reduces diastolic contractile dysfunction. [8] Treatment for unstable angina is aimed at pain reduction, limiting damage to the myocardium, and decreasing morbidity and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Prinzmetal Angina -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nicorandil, a nitrate, and K-channel activator also suppress vasospastic attacks.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrates : In patients with ongoing chest pain suggestive of myocardial infarction, administering sublingual nitroglycerin can help alleviate symptoms and improve coronary blood flow. The recommended dose is 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin, which can be repeated every 5 minutes for 3 doses if the pain persists.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitroglycerin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroglycerin is a vasodilatory drug used primarily to provide relief from anginal chest pain. It is currently FDA approved for the acute relief of an attack or acute prophylaxis of angina pectoris secondary to coronary artery disease. Off-label, non-FDA-approved uses include treatment for hypertensive urgency/emergency, coronary artery spasm, angina secondary to cocaine use, congestive heart failure (CHF), and chronic anal fissures. This activity outlines the indications, mechanism of action, methods of administration, important adverse effects, contraindications, monitoring, and toxicity of nitroglycerin, so providers can direct patient therapy successfully in instances where nitroglycerin benefits patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitroglycerin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroglycerin is a vasodilatory drug used primarily to provide relief from anginal chest pain. Nitroglycerin has been FDA approved since 2000 and was first sold as a brand-name agent. It is currently FDA approved for the acute relief of an attack or acute prophylaxis of angina pectoris secondary to coronary artery disease. Off-label, non-FDA-approved uses include treatment for hypertensive urgency/emergency, coronary artery spasm, angina secondary to cocaine use, congestive heart failure (CHF), and chronic anal fissures. Along with hydralazine as a combination therapy, it is also indicated in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection in whom ACE inhibitors are contraindicated. Nitroglycerin is occasionally given to reduce radial spasms during coronary angiography through the radial approach.  Nitroglycerin was first used in 1879 as a treatment for angina and since it has been part of management to relieve anginal chest pain. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Prinzmetal Angina -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The use of long-acting nitrates are also effective in preventing vasospastic events, but chronic use is associated with tolerance. In patients on calcium antagonist without an adequate response to treatment, long-acting nitrates can be added.

retrieved_docs.title: Stable Angina -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Symptom Control Beta-blockers: Beta-blockers have been shown to decrease heart rate, blood pressure, and contractility, leading to decreased myocardial oxygen demand and decreased anginal symptoms. [25] Nitrates: Nitrates relax vascular smooth muscle, leading to dilation of veins primarily; this decreases cardiac preload and, in turn, decreases myocardial oxygen demand, relieving anginal symptoms. [26] Ranolazine: The mechanism of action of this medication is not entirely understood; however, it is FDA-approved for symptom control in stable angina. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease Selection of Treatment Modality -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy for Relief of Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrates are potent vasodilators and cause a combination of venodilation (decreasing preload), arterioral dilation (decreasing afterload), as well as coronary dilation. It is the decreased preload and ventricular wall stress, with resultant decreased oxygen demand that primarily mediates its anti-anginal effects, however. Nitrates are available in sublingual form, which bypasses the first-pass effect and can be used acutely, while more sustained oral or transdermal formulations may be added to beta-blocker therapy for prolonged anti-anginal effect. This requires that a nitrate-free interval of at least 10 to 12 hours be maintained, usually overnight, to prevent tachyphylaxis and diminishing effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Angina -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: As the initial symptom of CAD in many cases, the main complication of angina is a future cardiac event, such as myocardial infarction. In one study, estimates are that the 10-year risk of MI exceeded 10 percent in women with chronic stable angina starting from when they initially began to use nitrates to treat symptoms. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease Prevention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Characteristic features of stable angina are chest pain that worsens with emotional and physical exertion, cold weather, and meals. The patient experiences pain relief from rest and nitrates. Features of unstable angina include pain at rest that lasts over 30 minutes and does not relieve with sublingual nitroglycerine. Pain associated with sweating, nausea, and vomiting may suggest myocardial infarction (MI). Patients may present with complications of MI such as heart failure, ventricular septal defect (VSD), harsh systolic murmur, or papillary muscle rupture.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Treatment / Management -- Nitroglycerin Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroglycerin is available in multiple formulations—intravenous, sublingual, transdermal, and oral—and acts by dilating coronary arteries to enhance blood flow and lower blood pressure. This vasodilation reduces myocardial workload and oxygen demand, helping to relieve ischemic symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrates -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The venodilation increases the venous capacitance and lowers the preload; this subsequently lowers the left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, resulting in a reduction in myocardium workload, which decreases the oxygen demand of the heart. [2] [6] At higher doses, nitrates can also exert significant coronary artery dilation, allowing blood flow to ischemic areas during coronary artery occlusion and vasospastic angina. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrates -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroglycerin (NTG) – angina pectoris (treatment/prophylaxis), acute coronary syndrome, heart failure, hypertension Isosorbide mononitrate (ISMN) – chronic angina pectoris (treatment) Isosorbide dinitrate (ISDN) – angina pectoris (treatment/prophylaxis) Pentaerythrityl tetranitrate (PETN) – Not commonly used in the United States Erythrityl tetranitrate (ETN) – Not commonly used in the United States

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Isosorbide mononitrate is classified as a member of the group of organic nitrates used in cardiac disease, a class of medications that provide nitrate‑mediated vasodilation and are employed in the treatment of cardiac conditions such as angina.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1582
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrates used in cardiac disease constitute a class of vasodilator medications that alleviate myocardial oxygen demand and improve coronary blood flow in conditions such as angina pectoris and heart failure. Isosorbiddinitrát is a member of this class; as an organic nitrate, it is employed to manage cardiac symptoms by releasing nitric oxide, which relaxes vascular smooth muscle and reduces cardiac preload and afterload. Consequently, isosorbiddinitrát exemplifies the therapeutic role of organic nitrates in the treatment of cardiac disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2368
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrates used in cardiac disease constitute a class of vasodilators employed in the management of cardiac conditions, and isosorbiddinitrát (isosorbide dinitrate) is a specific member of this organic nitrate group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2631
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrates used in cardiac disease constitute a class of vasodilators employed in the treatment of cardiac conditions, and isosorbide mononitrate is a member of this organic nitrate class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2584
retrieved_docs.content: Sildenafil and tadalafil are phosphodiesterase‑5 inhibitor medications that are contraindicated for use with nitroglycerin, a nitrate vasodilator; concurrent administration of either sildenafil or tadalafil with nitroglycerin can lead to severe hypotension and should be avoided in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
retrieved_docs.content: this agent. Overall, the hierarchical organization shows that agents targeting BPH, erectile dysfunction, and related urological conditions are grouped under urologicals within the genito‑urinary and sex hormone system, with mechanism‑based subclasses (alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists, testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors, phosphodiesterase‑5 inhibitors) defining therapeutic use, physiological effects, and safety considerations, while organic nitrates serve cardiac indications with specific contraindications and vasodilatory actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1569
retrieved_docs.content: Celiprolol produces vasodilation as its physiologic effect, a property that contributes to its therapeutic action in cardiovascular conditions such as hypertension and angina.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1670
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroglycerin is a pharmacologic agent whose primary physiologic effects include both arterial vasodilation and venous vasodilation, actions that reduce cardiac preload and afterload and are useful in the management of angina and other ischemic heart conditions. Because nitroglycerin can produce significant hypotension, it is contraindicated for use together with tadalafil, a phosphodiesterase‑5 inhibitor, as the combination may lead to dangerous drops in blood pressure. Additionally, the application of nitroglycerin in the context of pregnancy‑related toxicity (تسمم الحمل) requires special clinical judgment, as its safety and efficacy in this setting are not well established and must be weighed against potential maternal and fetal risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 383
retrieved_docs.content: nisoldipin, nilvadipine, mepiridipine, nifedipine, nikardipine and nitrendipine—share a common chemical scaffold and a unified mechanism of action: inhibition of L‑type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation, reduced peripheral vascular resistance, and consequent blood‑pressure reduction. This mechanism also underlies their utility in angina pectoris and other cardiovascular conditions, and it confers similar therapeutic effects, side‑effect profiles, and drug‑interaction patterns across the dihydropyridine group. Combination therapy that pairs an antihypertenziva with a diuretic, while not classified as a single antihypertenziva, is closely linked to the antihypertensive category because the diuretic component reduces plasma volume and the antihypertenziva component provides vascular relaxation, yielding synergistic blood‑pressure control. Understanding these relationships enables clinicians to select appropriate monotherapy or combination

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: delineate a hierarchical organization of cardiac nitrates, urological acidifiers, BPH therapies, erectile‑dysfunction agents, and related contraindications, providing a comprehensive framework for pharmacologic management in these therapeutic domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), abdominal pain (ألم بطني), and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). The vasoconstrictive and pro‑thrombotic effects of cocaine can precipitate acute myocardial ischemia, manifesting as unstable angina, while its sympathomimetic activity commonly raises systemic arterial pressure. Additionally, cocaine‑induced visceral vasospasm or ischemia may present as abdominal pain. Recognizing these interrelated clinical manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of patients who use cocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2221
retrieved_docs.content: Quinolone vasodilators used in cardiac diseases constitute a specific subclass of vasodilators used in cardiac diseases, indicating that they share the general therapeutic purpose of improving cardiac function through vessel relaxation while possessing distinct quinolone‑based chemical properties. Within this subclass, the drug flosequinan is identified as a member, exemplifying a quinolone vasodilator that is employed in the management of cardiac conditions. Recognizing flosequinan as part of the quinolone vasodilator group helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action and place it appropriately among other cardiac vasodilators for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 171
retrieved_docs.content: identification of the occluded coronary territory is emphasized to guide reperfusion and optimize therapeutic strategy in acute coronary syndromes, supporting accurate diagnosis, classification, and targeted management of acute ischemic heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1444
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrates used in cardiac disease are classified as vasodilators used in cardiac diseases, indicating that they function to widen blood vessels in order to improve cardiac perfusion. The specific compound erythrityltetranitrát belongs to this class of organic nitrates, exemplifying a member of the cardiac‑targeted vasodilator group. Clinical use of erythrityltetranitrát requires caution because it has a contraindicated drug interaction with زَرَق اِنْسِدادِ الزَّاوِيَّة, meaning that co‑administration of these two agents should be avoided to prevent adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: Amlodipine, clevidipine, levamlodipine, manidipine, mepirodipine, nifedipine, nimodipine, nitrendipine and cilnidipine are all members of the dihydropyridine derivative class. Dihydropyridine derivatives constitute a subgroup of calcium‑channel‑blocking agents that preferentially act on vascular smooth‑muscle L‑type calcium channels, producing vasodilation and lowering blood pressure. Because of their shared chemical scaffold, these drugs exhibit similar pharmacologic properties, such as rapid onset of action and a primary use in the management of hypertension and angina, while some agents (e.g., nimodipine) have additional indications such as prevention of cerebral vasospasm. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the dihydropyridine derivative family helps clinicians understand their common mechanisms, anticipate class‑related effects, and select appropriate therapy for cardiovascular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1767
retrieved_docs.content: Quinolone vasodilators used in cardiac diseases constitute a specific subclass of vasodilators used in cardiac diseases. Within this subclass, flosequinan is identified as a member, and its primary physiologic effect is the induction of vasodilation. Similarly, the beta‑blocker celiprolol also produces vasodilation as a physiologic effect. The shared vasodilatory action of both flosequinan and celiprolol highlights their relevance in therapeutic strategies aimed at reducing vascular resistance and improving cardiac function in patients with cardiac disease.
